intro.title: Start of Shift
intro.content: Your 12-hour day shift on 5 North is about to begin. It’s 07:00. The controlled chaos of shift change is buzzing around the nurses' station. You grab a coffee and find the night shift nurse to get your report. You're assigned four patients today.
intro.imageBrief: A busy nurses' station in a modern hospital. It's early morning, with fluorescent lighting. A diverse group of nurses in scrubs are talking, looking at computer monitors, and moving purposefully. In the foreground, one nurse is handing a tablet with patient charts to another nurse, representing the shift handoff.

scene1_prioritization.title: Morning Handoff & Prioritization
scene1_prioritization.content:
The night nurse gives you a quick report on your four patients:

1.  **Room 512:** Mrs. Vance, 88F, post-op day 2 for a hip fracture. Vitals stable, but she was moaning in pain most of the night. Her daughter, Sarah, is at the bedside and has refused all opioid pain medication for her mom, citing fears of addiction. The doctor has ordered IV Tylenol and oral ibuprofen only, per the daughter's request.
2.  **Room 514:** Mr. Jones, 65M, admitted yesterday for cellulitis of the left leg. On IV antibiotics. Stable.
3.  **Room 515:** Ms. Chen, 45F, post-op day 1 from an appendectomy. Tolerating a clear liquid diet, ambulating with assistance. She is awaiting discharge paperwork and education.
4.  **Room 518:** Mr. Henderson, 72M, admitted for community-acquired pneumonia. On oxygen via nasal cannula. Stable.

Based on the report, what is your first priority?
scene1_prioritization.imageBrief: A close-up shot over the shoulder of a nurse looking at a digital tablet. The tablet screen shows a patient list with four names and brief summaries, corresponding to Mrs. Vance, Mr. Jones, Ms. Chen, and Mr. Henderson. The nurse's hand is poised over the screen, indicating a decision is being made. The background is the slightly blurred nurses' station.

options.label: Start the discharge paperwork and education for Ms. Chen in 515 to open up a bed.
Go to Room 512 to perform a full head-to-toe assessment on Mrs. Vance and address her pain.
Quickly lay eyes on all four of your patients to ensure everyone is safe and stable before starting assessments.
Begin pulling all 09:00 medications from the {{Omnicell}} to get ahead of the morning med pass.

scene1_discharge_redirect.title: A Call Light Pulls You Back
scene1_discharge_redirect.content: You start toward the workstation to open Ms. Chen's discharge chart, but Mrs. Vance's call light goes off and her daughter waves from Room 512. Discharge planning matters, but it is not the safest first move when another patient had uncontrolled pain overnight. You redirect to assess Mrs. Vance before the morning gets away from you.
scene1_discharge_redirect.imageBrief: A hospital hallway outside the nurses' station. A nurse has one hand near a workstation with discharge paperwork on the monitor, while a call light for Room 512 is visibly flashing in the hallway. In the background, a worried family member stands near a patient room doorway, signaling that the nurse needs to reprioritize.

scene1_assess_pain_redirect.title: You Go Straight to Room 512
scene1_assess_pain_redirect.content: You prioritize the patient with the clearest active problem: uncontrolled post-op pain. That is clinically appropriate because pain can worsen mobility, breathing, delirium risk, and recovery. You gather your assessment supplies and head into Room 512 to evaluate Mrs. Vance and speak with Sarah.
scene1_assess_pain_redirect.imageBrief: Reuse the Room 512 bedside assessment image from public/scenes/scene2_assessment.jpg for this pain-prioritization redirect scene.

scene1_quick_look_redirect.title: Safety Rounds Confirm the Priority
scene1_quick_look_redirect.content: You do a rapid visual safety check on all four rooms. Mr. Jones and Mr. Henderson are breathing comfortably, and Ms. Chen is stable while waiting on paperwork. In Room 512, Mrs. Vance is visibly grimacing and Sarah looks worried, so your quick rounds correctly funnel you back to the pain-management problem.
scene1_quick_look_redirect.imageBrief: A four-panel hospital hallway view showing quick visual checks into patient rooms. Three rooms look calm and stable, while Room 512 stands out with an elderly patient grimacing and a worried daughter at bedside. The nurse is shown moving efficiently from the hallway toward Room 512 after the safety sweep.

scene1_meds_redirect.title: Assess Before You Medicate
scene1_meds_redirect.content: At the {{Omnicell}}, you realize you are about to let the task list drive the shift before you have assessed your patients. Morning medications are important, but safe nursing practice starts with knowing who is stable, who is changing, and who needs immediate assessment. You close the drawer and head to Room 512 first.
scene1_meds_redirect.imageBrief: A medication room with an Omnicell cabinet open and a nurse pausing before pulling medications. A small printed reminder near the workstation reads 'Assess before med pass.' The nurse is turning away from the cabinet toward the hallway, choosing patient assessment before task completion.

scene2_assessment.title: Bedside Assessment: Room 512
scene2_assessment.objective: Perform the focused bedside checks yourself before you decide how to advocate for Mrs. Vance.
scene2_assessment.simulatedAction: Focused post-op bedside assessment with hand hygiene, identity verification, bed safety, pain assessment, wound inspection, neurovascular check, breathing/mobility impact check, and order review.
scene2_assessment.content:
You enter Room 512 before the family conversation. The night report told you she was in pain, but safe nursing means verifying the patient, assessing the current picture, and looking for complications instead of acting from report alone.

**Before touching the patient or equipment, remember the first safety step at the doorway.**

Place Mrs. Vance's bed across the center-right from x 30-77%, y 28-76%, with her torso around x 38-58%, hip dressing around x 46-59%, wrist/bed rail around x 56-70%, and foot area around x 66-77%. Put the vitals monitor at x 72-90%, y 13-30%, a bedside table with pain scale at x 8-28%, y 65-83%, and a rolling workstation/order card at x 75-93%, y 57-85%. Sarah may be soft and out of focus in the far background; do not let her or large equipment cover the interaction zones.

scene2_clinical_prep.title: Prep: A Difficult Conversation
scene2_clinical_prep.content: Before you speak with Sarah, you pause at the bedside and organize the clinical facts you need.
scene2_clinical_prep.referenceContent:
**Situation**
- Mrs. Vance is 88 years old, post-op day 2 after a hip fracture.
- She is grimacing and rates pain as 8/10.
- Her current vitals are HR 115 and BP 168/92.
- Sarah is refusing opioids because addiction runs in the family.

**Why pain control matters**
- Severe acute pain can raise heart rate and blood pressure and make recovery harder.
- If Mrs. Vance cannot deep-breathe, cough, reposition, or get out of bed, her risk of complications such as pneumonia, blood clots, delirium, and delayed mobility can increase.
- Good pain management supports safe turning, breathing exercises, physical therapy, sleep, and participation in care.

**Medication plan facts**
- Addiction concern is clinically relevant family context, not something to dismiss.
- Short-term, closely monitored post-op pain treatment is a different clinical situation from unmanaged long-term opioid use.
- A multimodal plan may include scheduled acetaminophen/NSAID if appropriate, ice, repositioning, and the lowest effective opioid dose only if needed.
- Opioid-related safety monitoring includes sedation, breathing, confusion, constipation, fall risk, and pain score reassessment.
- Mrs. Vance, Sarah, the nurse, and the prescriber all have roles in choosing a safe pain-control plan.

scene2_conversation.title: A Difficult Conversation
scene2_conversation.content:
Sarah is still at the bedside, looking worried and protective. As you explain that you'd like to help manage her mother's pain, Sarah interrupts you. 'Absolutely no narcotics,' she says firmly. 'Addiction runs in our family, and I will not let her get hooked on that stuff at her age.'

Use the clinical facts you just reviewed to speak with Sarah using therapeutic communication and plain-language clinical evidence. Your goal is to explain why pain management matters while respecting her concerns.
scene2_conversation.imageBrief: A first-person bedside view inside Room 512, a clean modern hospital room. An elderly female patient, Mrs. Vance, is in bed looking uncomfortable while her middle-aged daughter Sarah stands close to the bedside and speaks directly toward the nurse's point of view with a worried, protective expression. Include subtle hospital-room details like bed rails, an IV pole, a vitals monitor, and soft clinical lighting without making the equipment overwhelming.
scene2_conversation.goalPrompt: You are Sarah Vance, the anxious daughter of an 88-year-old patient. Your mother is in pain, but you are terrified of her becoming addicted to opioids because "addiction runs in the family". You are firm that she should only receive ibuprofen. Your goal is to resist the nurse's attempts to provide stronger pain medication. Listen to the nurse's rationale. If they explain the low risk of addiction at this age, the importance of pain control for healing/mobility, and offer a multi-modal approach (e.g., a small dose of a less strong opioid combined with other meds), you can be cautiously persuaded. If they are dismissive or aggressive, become more resistant. After 2-3 exchanges where the nurse has made their case, conclude the conversation by saying either "Okay... I'm still not sure, but I'll think about what you said. Let's see what the doctor says," or "No, I'm not comfortable with that. We will stick to the ibuprofen."

scene3_orders.title: The Order Barrage
scene3_orders.content:
You're finally sitting down to chart your assessments. You've documented Mrs. Vance's pain and your conversation with her daughter. Suddenly, an alert for a new {{STAT}} order from Dr. Carter pops up in the {{EMR}}: '{{STAT}} Lactate' for Mrs. Vance. You acknowledge the order, go into the room, draw the blood, and send it to the lab. As you sit back down, another {{STAT}} order appears: '{{STAT}} Troponin'. You groan, knowing you could have drawn both at the same time.

Fifteen minutes later, a third {{STAT}} order for a 'CBC' appears. You need to communicate with Dr. Carter efficiently and professionally to address this pattern, which is causing unnecessary discomfort for the patient and wasting time.

scene4_stabilize.title: Room 514: Stabilize First
scene4_stabilize.objective: Act at the bedside before you write the SBAR.
scene4_stabilize.simulatedAction: Immediate bedside deterioration response: hand hygiene, gather full vitals and assess the acute change, call the senior nurse into the room with role delegation, raise the head of bed in a closeup, connect and apply oxygen with flow setting, activate rapid response, and write scratch facts for the later formal SBAR.
scene4_stabilize.content: You're in Mr. Jones's room to hang his next IV antibiotic, but you do not hang the antibiotic yet. He is newly confused, picking at the blanket, breathing fast, and his oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. Marco is walking past the doorway. Foam in, assess the acute change and vitals, call Marco in with a clear role, raise the head of bed, start oxygen, activate rapid response, and write the facts you will later turn into a formal SBAR.

Put bed head controls at x 17-35%, y 62-80% for a closeup popup launch. Put the vital-sign monitor at x 72-92%, y 9-31% with visible SpO2 88%, RR 28, HR 122, BP 96/58, temp 38.9 C. scene4_decline.title: Formal SBAR From Scratch Facts
scene4_decline.content: You stabilized first in Room 514 and wrote rough scratch facts at the bedside. Now turn those facts into a formal SBAR report for Marco and the rapid response team.
scene4_decline.prompt: Use the scratch facts from stabilization to write a concise formal SBAR. Include the acute change, relevant background, current assessment data, support already started, and what you need next.

appTabs.label: Hints
appTabs.content:
**SBAR support facts**

**Background that may not be obvious from the playground**
- Mr. Jones is a 65-year-old patient in Room 514.
- He was admitted yesterday for left-leg cellulitis and is receiving IV antibiotics.
- He was previously stable; you entered to hang his next IV antibiotic, but you paused because of the acute change.

**Assessment facts to pull into SBAR**
- New confusion/agitation and rapid, shallow breathing.
- SpO2 88% on room air.
- RR 28, HR 122, BP 96/58, temp 38.9 C.

**Actions already started**
- Stayed with the patient and called Marco into the room.
- Raised the head of bed to support breathing.
- Started nasal-cannula oxygen at 2-4 L/min.
- Activated rapid response for acute hypoxia, confusion, and respiratory distress.

**What the recommendation should ask for**
- Ask the rapid response team/provider for urgent bedside evaluation and continued support for hypoxia, respiratory distress, and possible sepsis or clinical deterioration.

You have assessed her pain and mobility risk, spoken with Sarah, completed the ordered non-opioid medication pass and reassessment plan, coordinated with Dr. Carter about repeated {{STAT}} labs, and completed the CBC draw workflow. Use the reference notes and hints to turn that shift story into a structured handoff.

appTabs.label: Hints
appTabs.content:
**SBAR support facts**

**Background that may not be obvious from the playground**
- Mrs. Vance is an 88-year-old patient in Room 512, post-op day 2 after hip-fracture repair.
- She had severe pain overnight; Sarah, her daughter, is anxious about opioids because addiction runs in the family.
- The current ordered plan is non-opioid pain control with IV acetaminophen and PO ibuprofen.

**Assessment facts to pull into SBAR**
- Earlier pain was 8/10 with grimacing, HR 115, BP 168/92, shallow breathing, and guarding with turning.
- Surgical dressing was dry/intact and neurovascular checks were reassuring.
- Pain control matters for deep breathing, repositioning, mobility, sleep, and delirium/complication prevention.

**Actions already completed**
- Spoke with Sarah using clinical reasoning and respectful family education.
- Completed medication safety checks, administered the ordered non-opioid medications, and set reassessment reminders for pain and side effects.
- Messaged Dr. Carter about repeated piecemeal STAT lab orders; Dr. Carter confirmed the CBC was the last anticipated STAT lab for now.
- Verified the STAT CBC order and patient ID, explained the draw, selected the correct supplies, drew the lavender-top CBC tube, labeled it at bedside, disposed of sharps, and sent the specimen.

**What the recommendation should ask for**
- Reassess pain and side effects on schedule and escalate if pain remains uncontrolled or prevents breathing, repositioning, or mobility.
- Follow up on the STAT CBC result and any provider response.
- Keep Sarah informed while continuing to advocate for safe, adequate pain control.

scene2_explain_meds.title: Explain the Medications
scene2_explain_meds.content: Before administration, explain the ordered medications to Mrs. Vance in plain language. Use the notes as a reminder, but speak naturally rather than reading them word-for-word.
scene2_explain_meds.playerGoal: Explain what IV acetaminophen and PO ibuprofen are for, how they will be given, what she should report, and that you will reassess her response.
scene2_explain_meds.prepReferenceContent:
- IV acetaminophen: non-opioid pain medicine, through IV
- PO ibuprofen: anti-inflammatory pain medicine, taken with water
- Expected: pain relief, easier deep breathing / movement
- Ask Mrs. Vance to tell you if she feels: nausea, dizziness, rash, trouble breathing, worse pain, stomach discomfort
- Tell Mrs. Vance you will reassess: pain score + side effects after administration
scene2_explain_meds.meetingContext: Room 512. Mrs. Vance is awake, uncomfortable, and ready for her scheduled non-opioid pain medication pass. The nurse has already completed the safety checks and scans.
scene2_explain_meds.goalPrompt: You are Mrs. Eleanor Vance, an elderly post-op hip-fracture patient receiving scheduled non-opioid pain medication. Listen to the nurse's explanation. If the nurse clearly explains both medications, their purpose, how they will be given, what symptoms or side effects to report, and that they will reassess your pain/response, respond briefly: "I understand. Thank you for explaining." Do not ask extra questions after a clear explanation. If the explanation is vague, missing one medication, or uses too much jargon, ask one focused follow-up question such as "What is the IV medicine for?" or "What should I tell you if I feel strange?" Give the nurse up to two chances to clarify. If it is still unclear after two follow-up questions, say: "Could you show me the medication note instead? I am still not quite following." Stay polite, tired, and realistic.

scene2_reassessment_reminder.title: Set Reassessment Reminder
scene2_reassessment_reminder.learningGoal: Plan a realistic post-medication reassessment for pain relief and side effects.
scene2_reassessment_reminder.simulatedAction: Set an eMAR reminder interval and select the clinical findings to reassess after pain medication administration.
scene2_reassessment_reminder.content: Medication administration is not finished until you plan the follow-up. Set a reminder for reassessment and choose what you will check when you return.
scene2_reassessment_reminder.postStageHint:
Set two reminders: first, an IV-med reassessment at about 30 minutes to catch early response or side effects; second, a later oral-med follow-up at about 60 minutes for the ibuprofen response.

After pain medication, plan what you will check when you return:
- Did her pain score improve?
- Is she more sleepy, confused, or hard to wake?
- Is her breathing still comfortable?
- Does she have nausea, stomach discomfort, rash, or other side effects?
- Is the IV site and line still okay?
- Can she deep-breathe, reposition, or move a little more comfortably?

scene3_explain_draw.playerGoal: Explain that the CBC is the last anticipated STAT lab for now, that you verified the order and identifiers, that labeling will happen at the bedside, and that you are batching the work to avoid more unnecessary sticks.
scene3_explain_draw.prepReferenceContent:
- The visible order is a STAT CBC for Mrs. Vance.
- Dr. Carter confirmed this is the last anticipated STAT lab for now.
- Two identifiers and bedside labeling protect against specimen errors.
- Mrs. Vance is tired and frustrated by repeated interruptions.
scene3_explain_draw.meetingContext: Room 512. Mrs. Vance is tired after repeated STAT lab interruptions and is worried this will be another painful, piecemeal stick.
scene3_explain_draw.goalPrompt: You are Mrs. Eleanor Vance, an elderly post-op patient who is tired and frustrated by repeated blood draws. Listen to the nurse. If the nurse clearly explains why the CBC is needed, that this should be the last anticipated STAT draw for now, that they verified your identity/order, and that they will label the tube at bedside, relax a little and say you are ready. If the nurse is vague, uses too much jargon, or does not acknowledge the repeated interruptions, ask one focused follow-up question such as "Is this going to happen again?" or "How do I know this tube is mine?" Stay polite but weary.

scene4_call_marco.content: Call Marco into the room from the bedside and give the immediate reason for help.
scene4_call_marco.playerGoal: Get Marco into Room 514 immediately by naming the acute change: Mr. Jones is newly confused, breathing fast, and hypoxic at SpO2 88% on room air. Ask Marco to help escalate or trigger rapid response, gather support/vitals, or stay engaged while you raise the head of bed and start oxygen.
scene4_call_marco.prepReferenceContent:
- Room 514: Mr. Jones, cellulitis on IV antibiotics.
- New confusion and rapid shallow breathing.
- SpO2 88% on room air.
- Ask Marco for a concrete role before positioning and oxygen: help activate rapid response, get another set of eyes/vitals, or stay with you while you raise the head of bed and start oxygen.
scene4_call_marco.meetingContext: Room 514. Marco is passing the doorway while Mr. Jones is newly confused and hypoxic. This is an urgent in-person escalation with quick role delegation, not a formal SBAR yet.
scene4_call_marco.goalPrompt: You are Marco Diaz, senior RN, passing Room 514. If the nurse clearly calls you in, names the acute safety concern, and gives you a concrete role such as helping activate rapid response, gathering support/vitals, or staying engaged while they raise the head of bed and start oxygen, step into the room and respond bluntly but helpfully: "I am here. Raise him upright and start oxygen. I will help get rapid response moving." If the nurse is vague or does not convey urgency, ask, "What changed? Give me the quick facts and what you need me to do." Keep responses short and focused on bedside action.

priya.name: Priya Sharma
priya.persona: Priya is a South Asian woman in her late 30s with a calm, authoritative presence. She is efficient, fair, and unflappable. While supportive of new nurses, she expects competence and clear communication. Priya is constantly managing staffing levels and patient flow for the entire unit, so she needs her team to be reliable and to ask for help before a situation becomes a crisis.

marco.name: Marco Diaz
marco.persona: A Filipino man in his 50s, Marco has worked on 5 North for 20 years. He has a gruff exterior and a low tolerance for incompetence, but is highly skilled and secretly proud of his work. He is wary of overconfident new grads but respects those who are willing to learn and value his extensive experience. His communication style is blunt and often filled with nursing jargon.

ben.name: Dr. Ben Carter
ben.persona: A white man in his late 20s, Dr. Carter is smart, fast-talking, and well-intentioned, but often appears disorganized and overwhelmed. He is juggling a dozen patients across multiple floors and is under immense pressure from his attending physician to have all the data, all the time. He communicates in rapid-fire messages and can be difficult to reach by phone.

sarah.name: Sarah Vance
sarah.persona: Sarah is a white woman in her late 50s who is anxious, intelligent, and skeptical of the healthcare system. Deeply devoted to her mother, she feels guilty about her mother's fall and is terrified of making a wrong decision that could cause more harm. She is particularly fearful of her mother developing an addiction to pain medication and questions medical advice based on things she has 'read online'.

eleanor.name: Mrs. Eleanor Vance
eleanor.persona: Mrs. Vance is an 88-year-old post-op hip-fracture patient. She is tired and in significant pain but alert, polite, and trying to cooperate. She appreciates plain language, reassurance, and knowing what to report after medication. She may ask simple clarifying questions if an explanation is vague or rushed.

intro.welcomeBody: You are a new graduate Registered Nurse, four months into your first job at Metropolitan University Health System (MUHS), a large urban teaching hospital. You'll be working on 5 North, a busy Medical-Surgical floor, where you'll manage patient care, make critical decisions, and collaborate with the healthcare team.

steps.label: The Company
steps.content: Metropolitan University Health System (MUHS) is a leading 800-bed urban teaching hospital. It provides comprehensive patient care, from routine procedures to level 1 trauma, and is dedicated to training the next generation of healthcare professionals.

steps.label: Your Role
steps.content: As a new Registered Nurse on the 5 North Medical-Surgical unit, you're responsible for assessing patients, administering treatments, coordinating with the multidisciplinary team, and ensuring patient safety. You'll start your shift by getting a report on your assigned patients.

steps.label: The Challenge
steps.content: Over your 12-hour shift, you'll face common nursing challenges: prioritizing care, handling difficult conversations with family members, communicating with physicians, and responding to a patient's sudden decline. Your clinical judgment and communication skills will be put to the test.

glossary.Med-Surg: Medical-Surgical, a hospital unit that cares for adult patients with a wide variety of illnesses and those recovering from surgery.
glossary.SBAR: A structured communication tool for healthcare professionals: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation.
glossary.EMR: Electronic Medical Record; the digital version of a patient's chart.
glossary.STAT: A medical term derived from the Latin statim, meaning immediately or urgently.
glossary.pulse oximeter: A medical device that measures the oxygen saturation level in the blood.
glossary.Rapid Response Team (RRT): A dedicated team of critical care providers that can be called to the bedside to assist with a patient who is rapidly declining.
glossary.handoff: The process of transferring patient information and responsibility from one healthcare provider to another, typically at the end of a shift.
glossary.Omnicell: An automated medication dispensing cabinet used in hospitals to securely store and track medications.

workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: Med-surg bedside nursing is dominated by direct patient care, procedural safety checks, clinical prioritization, documentation, and face-to-face communication. The simulator therefore puts the heaviest active weight on visual bedside playgrounds for assessment, medication safety, lab draw setup, and acute stabilization, while still preserving spoken family, patient, and peer conversations plus written SBAR update work.

sourceEvidence: O*NET and collected nursing sources emphasize direct care, monitoring patient status, administering medications, collecting specimens, operating bedside equipment, and responding to changing conditions.
The approved nurse story centers on bedside assessment, med pass, lab draw setup, and acute deterioration response.
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Most clinically consequential active scenes are physical_playground stages where the player manipulates bedside tools, labels, monitors, supplies, controls, and patient-room objects.

sourceEvidence: Nursing work requires prioritizing competing patients, recognizing deterioration, sequencing safety checks, and deciding when to escalate.
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: Prioritization, clinical sequencing, and escalation decisions are embedded inside the playground steps and SBAR documentation.

sourceEvidence: The role includes charting, concise secure messages, SBAR notes, patient updates, and shift handoff documentation.
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: The simulator includes a secure chat to the physician, a Mrs. Vance SBAR update, and a structured SBAR report for Mr. Jones.

sourceEvidence: Bedside nursing includes family education, patient explanations, urgent peer escalation, and therapeutic communication.
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: The simulator includes in-person voice conversations with the patient family member, patient, and senior RN during bedside care.

sourceEvidence: The shift involves interruptions, waiting for orders/results, reprioritizing after alerts, and context switching between patients.
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Passive/context-switch pressure appears as scenario timing and interruptions, but active scenes focus on the decisions and actions that follow.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: The active scene mix follows this med-surg nurse profile: visual bedside playgrounds carry the major procedural workload; voice meetings cover family education, patient explanation, and urgent peer escalation; written scenes capture secure messaging and SBAR updates; MCQ is limited to bounded shift prioritization.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: This role has much more direct patient/procedural work than a desk-tool job, so the simulator makes bedside manipulation and inspection the largest active component without applying a universal numeric ratio.

criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 申し送り情報から、緊急度、転倒や呼吸リスク、時間指定タスクを見て最初に見る患者を選べている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 安全上の必須条件を飛ばした場合は、他の要素ができていても満点にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。
